text: The surgeon places incisions in a vertical line along the left rectus muscle. The suction irrigator is used for gentle and blunt dissection, revealing the anatomy. The sigmoid colon appears tethered to the right lower quadrant, and gradual dissection allows identification of the terminal ileum entering the cecum.

The surgeon identifies the base of the appendix by observing the coalescence of the tinea. A small fissure in the inflamed tissue reveals the body of the appendix. Using a suction irrigator, the surgeon separates the ligament of Treves from the appendix, gradually elevating it from the surrounding inflammatory tissue. The curved monopolar scissors are then used to dissect the appendix free, creating a space between the appendix and the mesoappendix. The mesoappendix is visible to the left, and the surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to cauterize the appendiceal artery, which is then transected with the monopolar scissors. Some bleeding occurs but is quickly controlled.

The robotic console's wrists allow precise manipulation within the dissected cavity. The surgeon bluntly dissects the remainder of the appendix base from the cecum. Due to inflammation, two simple sutures are placed at the appendix base without imbricating the stump. The surgical field is irrigated to clear clots, focusing on the dependent portions of the pelvis.

The endo-catch bag is deployed from the superior port site, which is dilated to facilitate extraction. A drain is inserted through the inferior port site, draped into the pelvis over the surgical field. The dilated extraction port is then closed at the fascia with a vicryl suture.
